sections.heading: Understanding Vowel Sounds
sections.body:
Vowels are the heart of English pronunciation. There are 12 vowel sounds in English (not just 5 letters!):

Monophthongs (single sounds): /iː/ (see), /ɪ/ (sit), /e/ (bed), /æ/ (cat), /ɑː/ (car), /ɒ/ (hot), /ɔː/ (caught), /ʊ/ (put), /uː/ (food), /ʌ/ (cut), /ɜː/ (bird), /ə/ (about)

Diphthongs (gliding sounds): /eɪ/ (day), /aɪ/ (my), /ɔɪ/ (boy), /aʊ/ (now), /əʊ/ (go), /ɪə/ (near), /eə/ (care), /ʊə/ (tour)

Tip: Record yourself and compare with native speakers. Apps like Forvo can help.

sections.heading: Consonant Clusters
sections.body:
Some consonants combine in English:

Initial clusters: /bl/ (blue), /br/ (brown), /st/ (stop), /str/ (strong), /spr/ (spring)

Final clusters: /-st/ (fast), /-nd/ (hand), /-lk/ (walk), /-mps/ (lamps)

Common mistakes:
• "s" + consonant: "school" not "eschool"
• "str" cluster: "street" — practice all three sounds
• Final "d" and "t" are often silent in casual speech

sections.heading: Word Stress Patterns
sections.body:
English is a stress-timed language. Some syllables are louder and longer:

Nouns (stress 1st syllable): TA-ble, STU-dent, HOS-pi-tal
Verbs (stress 2nd syllable): re-PORT, be-GIN, de-CIDE
Adjectives (stress 2nd syllable): beAU-ti-ful, com-PU-ter

Two-syllable nouns vs verbs:
• RE-cord (noun) vs re-CORD (verb)
• PRE-sent (noun) vs pre-SENT (verb)
• PRO-ject (noun) vs pro-JECT (verb)

sections.heading: Practice Drills
sections.body:
1. Alphabet song — sing along daily to internalize letter sounds
2. Minimal pairs: ship/sheep, bed/bad, full/fool — listen for the difference
3. Read aloud for 10 minutes daily from any English text
4. Shadow native speakers: play audio, speak simultaneously
5. Tongue twisters:
• "She sells seashells by the seashore"
• "Peter Piper picked a peck of pickled peppers"
• "How much wood would a woodchuck chuck"

summary: English pronunciation takes practice. Focus on vowel sounds first — they carry the meaning. Record yourself weekly to track improvement.
